sentences.text: Well, one-- one situation which is really closer to home, since I married my wife Marianne.
Her father was thrown in jail by [?
Goya. ?] He owned-- my father-in-law-- owned several pieces of property, and the Japanese wanted to buy it.
And-- but his partners were-- was a Russian and an Indian, a Sikh Indian.
So the decision was not only my father-in-law's to sell the property.
So they tried to force him to sell the property by throwing him in jail.
But apparently-- and he was there several weeks-- and my mother-in-law-- well, Marianne's mother, I never knew the lady-- had a heart condition.
And that worsened her condition, and she eventually died in Shanghai.
It was a very sad situation.
I didn't know Mr.
Turner at the time, but this is what my wife told me happened to him.
The property was outside of the district.
And exactly, I don't know, whether it was in the district or outside of the district.
That I don't know, because he owned several.
But the conditions in jail were supposed to have been horrible.
Very, very good with the Chinese.
That some of the Chinese learned to speak German.
It was a friendly atmosphere.
The only ones who were not very friendly were the Chinese who collaborated with the Japanese on the Wang Jingwei who was the puppet regime.
The Chinese were put in uniforms similar to those that Japanese wore, so they were the ones who showed their muscle.
But the general population were happy that we were there, because they made a living from a lot of the immigrants.
We had five refugee camps, different schools and where people lived.
It was-- it became a very strong Jewish life.
We had a Jewish school.
I believe even two.
Synagogue, rabbis.
We were active in Jewish organizations, boy scouts, Zionist organizations.
I was a member from the left to the right, whether it was [HEBREW] or Betar.
So we-- we made the best of it.
Betar was later on forbidden.
We were not allowed to-- to meet, but we met anyway.
None whatsoever.
None whatsoever.
A very close friend of mine, who was there without his parents, and spent many of hours together, ate in our house many, many times, and when the war was over, I met his uncle.
And I said where is-- and I don't remember his first name.
He says, very tragically, he hung himself.
He found out his parents were killed, and he hung himself.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So we did not know.
Nobody knew, at least not in the ghetto.
During the time in the ghetto, did you receive communication from members of your family who did not leave Germany?
Not-- not during the ghetto period.
We still had correspondence from my grandparents in 1939, '40.
But as soon as the war broke out, everything was shut off.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
We lived in one room.
The toilet was in the same room.
The kitchen was in the same room.
My grandmother cooked on a hibachi stove for seven, eight years.
And-- or six years, maybe-- and it was a very, very simple life.
And there was times when I was hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Perhaps at the time it didn't matter so much.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My father did not want to have any help from the Jewish organizations.
He was just too proud.
Eventually he broke down, I think, in '44, and we-- we received some food.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Sugar was at a premium.
I think one US dollar or more a pound, which was a lot of money in those days.
And everything was rationed.
We-- my grandmother sometimes arranged to get a-- a cake from one of her relatives who had a bakery.
But we managed.
We stayed together.
[DRINKS]
I don't understand--
--the question.
The volunteer group, well, there was one.
I don't know if-- the-- the-- called the Poa-Chia.
Was a group of volunteers who quasi aided the authorities in-- in keeping people in the district.
And they-- they-- they wore armbands, or they were put on strategic points of the-- of the district.
And-- excuse me.
Not-- not very well liked people, but--
They were-- they were Jews.
Yeah, we all had to do it.
We were-- had to-- had to-- had to perform that-- that duty.
But couldn't wait to get out of it, you know.
It was just something that the Japanese forced us to do.
Perhaps some people even liked it.
Who knows.
Yeah.
Right.
Right.
No, I think we had to do it once a week.
No, any-- any age.
Any age group.
No.
No.
Well, first of all, it was-- I believe it was a Friday night when we were all-- and it was a hot night.
We all walked to the streets, because the apartment or the-- the rooms were just too hot.
No air conditioning in those days.
When it went through the streets like wildfire, the war was over.
And, in fact, I was walking with my parents at the time, my grandmother.
And so the-- the thought came to me immediately, I'll get in touch with my members of the Betar, of the Zionist group, and see what-- what actions we can take.
And so we arranged to meet the next morning early, about 6:00 in the morning, at the police station, the Japanese occupied police station.
And to see if we can find any kind of documents.
Well, unbeknownst to us, by the time we got there, they vanished.
Everything was gone.
All the documents.
The military was gone.
The police was gone.
Everything was gone.
Still no word of any Holocaust occurring in Europe.
That filtered through over the next few days and weeks.
Until mail came in and the Russian Jews in the-- in the city, who were never affected by the ghetto-- by the ghetto experience, then eventually they got word through their Russian sources.
But when exactly, I couldn't tell you when we actually found out what has happened in Europe.
Because the war in Germany was still on.
And Germany capitulated six months later, I believe.
Yeah, but the-- but the-- the-- the airwaves were-- were-- were jammed.
Occasionally that you would-- there was some shortwave receivers, but you couldn't hear much.
You couldn't hear much.
Now, people in the city, they were aware of it through various sources, because some of the consulates were open.
The Swiss consulate, the German consulate.
Well, the first thing is to-- to see if-- go into the city, see if find a job.
Then, of course, we were told the Americans are going to be here shortly.
Then a bunch of us would pilgrim out or-- or get out to the-- to the airbase, which was in not far from Hongkou.
We just walked out there or hiked out there, waiting for the planes.
Every day we'd go and watch and see if the planes would come in.
Finally, I-- I think about a week or two after that, the planes came in.
And I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when-- when the planes landed one of the pilots threw out a package out of the cockpit.
And it was a sea ration.
And so we tore it open, ate whatever we could see.
There was some concentrated chocolate, what have you.
I got dysentery immediately.
It went right through me and was just too potent.
Because it was-- I only weighed about 125 pounds, 130.
At that point I was 21, 22.
No wait a minute.
I was-- '45.
I was-- I was 24.
I met my wife after the war.
I met her in 1947.
We dated for three weeks and we were engaged.
And three weeks after that we got married.
We knew each other six weeks.
And it lasted so far 49 years.
She was also in the ghetto, but we saw each other.
But never-- I mean, not personally.
We'd-- you know, when you live that close, it's only about a-- maybe a-- a two or three mile square area, the whole ghetto.
So you always met at a certain street called Chusan Road that was the-- the-- the Main Street where-- where people congregated and-- and talked, and-- and met.
We also had-- had good times during the ghetto period.
It wasn't all bad.
We had coffee houses.
We had restaurants that opened up.
We had huts, hot dog stands.
We had opera.
We had theater.
We had concerts.
We had symphony.
So it wasn't-- there-- there was a cultural life.
But some people fared quite well, and others didn't.
People had businesses in the district, had shops in the district.
But we were not that fortunate.
My wife's family, thank god, they did better.
Well, naturally we all knew we couldn't stay in Shanghai.
The Communists also started making noises up in the north.
And we knew we-- there's no future for us.
Again, the same question came up, the same anxieties that we had once in Germany, where-- where we going to go?
Who was going to take us?
Go back to Europe?
Go to Palestine?
And several of my friends went illegally at that time, because it was still a British mandate.
Some of them were caught in Cyprus on the way to Palestine.
And although I was a Zionist, to be honest with you, I was too selfish to go through that again.
And I had a cousin who lived in Australia.
And I wrote to him immediately, and he arranged, actually, for me to get-- I had-- oh.
I forgot, I had a permit to go to Australia in 1939 that had arrived in Shanghai.
But it was only for me, and not for my parents, so I declined.
And when the war broke out, the next morning I went to the Australian consulate, and they said, sorry, you are a German subject.
So I couldn't go to-- to Australia.
And then after the war, many of my friends already left for the US, for South America, different countries.
And again the same question came up.
Fortunately, Australia was a possibility.
And then I heard about the United States, that under President Truman, a cooperative affidavit would be issued to young men who had trades and who could apply.
And I applied.
And because of my profession, I was able to get the visa to come the US.
Also, I was fortunate that I was a German quota, because in those days there was a quota system in the US, and the German quota was wide open.
And my wife-- we were married one year.
Although she was born in Austria, but she traveled on my quota.
And-- and my parents were able to come in because they were at a certain age where they received a permit to come in.
So that's how we finally ended up in the US in 1948.
Excuse me.
It was again in April of 1948.
We came into San Francisco on a ship called the General Meigs, which was an American [? present ?] line, a converted troop transport, men and women separate.
My wife and I, we found a spot where to meet.
And my parents were already in the US about three months, four months earlier.
My wife and I decided not to leave yet, because we were not in a big hurry, because my grandmother was still alive.
She was born in Prague, Czechoslovakia, and could not get a visa to come to the US.
So we had to make arrangements for her to go back to Germany, to an old age home at first.
And once she was all taken care of and she-- she left, that's when we came to the US.
And we settled-- we first came through San Francisco, like all of us.
Everybody threw the penny at-- in San Francisco, in the Bay under the bridge.
And we stayed there for a month, again thanks to the Jewish organizations.
Because I only had $30 in my pocket when we arrived.
There was HIAS.
We got stamps to eat in the cafeteria.
They put us up in a-- in a hotel in San Francisco.
And we stayed there for a month getting-- getting used to the American way of life.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Some experience.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] First time in-- in-- in my life that I remembered where I could be free.
Terrific.
And flap my wings.
And my parents already lived in Minneapolis.
And they arranged for-- naturally it was a foregone conclusion that we would be wherever they are.
And they rented a-- a one room with a kitchen privilege from a Jewish family, the Gordons.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we arrived on a Saturday.
On the following Wednesday, I had my first job.
In Minneapolis.
And never been without work since.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Just gets to me.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And I immediately stayed in my own profession.
And that's all I did in the United States.
I worked for a company in-- in Minneapolis for six months for $1 an hour.
My wife made $0.75 an hour.
I-- I was working for a company called Bloom Brothers in their office.
It was a leather goods company.
And I wanted to get into the factory.
Actually, I wanted to be a foreman, because I was already a foreman at times in Shanghai, in leather goods.
They said no.
The-- the foreman is 35 years old that we have now, and when he retires you can get his job.
So I said thanks.
So I-- I got a job with a big company, Brown and Bigelow, in Minneapolis.
I worked for them for about 3 and 1/2 years.
And then learned a lot.

From there, I moved on to Springfield, Ohio, where I took a job as a plant manager, another four years. 1952 to 1956. '56 we came out to California, because a company here hired me to run their plant.
And worked there for two years and then started on my own.
But I think I wanted to say something about Shanghai yet.
All the work that I've done in Shanghai.
That I think would like to have on record.
During the war, I worked on-- in bakeries.
I delivered coal from house to house.
All kinds of odd jobs.
I was a night watchman in a Japanese school.
We're talking about '42, '43 until '45.
During the height of the war.
And I even dealt on the black market--
--just stay alive.
Exchange dollars, and all kinds of odd jobs.
My dad was a broken man.
He-- he just couldn't work.
Uh-huh.
Well, there was times when my mom worked as a home worker, also.
Even as a-- as a matter of fact, lampshades.
She did that.
And so did my grandmother, during the war.
But anyway, life began in the US.
All of our children are born in the United States.
Two of them in Minneapolis, Minnesota, and two of them in Springfield, Ohio.
1956, following a job.
And then in 1958, like I said, I started my own small business in my-- in my garage in Sherman Oaks.
Manufacturing in leather goods.
And, well, the company at that time, actually, was Matzdorff House of Leather.
And one little episode, while I was working in my garage, and my neighbor and I, we shared our driveway.
And one day Al came home and he looked at me, and says, what are you doing here?
And I said, well, I'm making some samples and some wallets.
He says, well, jokingly, said you can't work here, this is in a-- it's a residential area.
I said, I know, I'm going to be moving out soon.
But he says, by the way, could you make me some card cases for business cards?
I said sure.
How many do you need?
He said, oh, about 1,000.
So I said, OK, I'll let you know.
What kind of leather?
Black, and leather, and so on.
So next day I showed him the leather and I made him a sample and gave a price of $0.75 each.
And he says, OK, give-- ship them to me whenever you can.
I said fine.
That was an order for $750.
And I said, Al, can you pay me in advance?
He did.
And that was part of my working capital.
So then I took my samples up to San Francisco.
I mean, do you want to-- like to hear about my business?
Sure.
Uh-huh.
And I took some samples up there, got an order. $300 and some dollars.
Based on that, I opened up a-- I-- I rented a storefront in Los Angeles on Pico Boulevard and started to work.
And people then eventually heard about me.
Even-- even salesmen from-- who worked for Brown and Bigelow in Minnesota, who lived here in California, they heard about me, that I moved here.
And they contacted me.
And little by little, it grew.
Wasn't easy.
Children were little.
Our youngest daughter was born in '55.
She was only a year old when we came to California.
By that time she was three.
And so was-- it wasn't-- wasn't easy with four little kids.
And my wife went to work.
She took a job in a hospital.
She was never worked before and helped out.
She worked also in the-- in the factory, my little shop.
Then my parents retired in Minneapolis July of '58, when I started.
And so it became a family affair.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
OK.
